author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: In regards to FIC (or any meta loop), there has to be more RNG, otherwise it's a race to the bottom.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: something along the lines of critical success, and/or critical failure when crafting, with subsequent reward (critical success), or consequence (critical failure) when the proc occurs. Ideally, this would be directly tied into a crafting skill tree that can be levelled up (maybe already in the works, i dunno)

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: profit needs to occur through markup sales between players, not through emissions. Would love to see a DAO merchant that sells "limited" ships that decay with time, with option to buy back the ship at below 100% decay at a net loss to the player, and net profit for the DAO (or ATMTA). This could extend to basic resources, where the DAO essentially can sell some basic resources, with option to buy back at a net loss to the player (profit for DAO or ATMTA). players wanting to test the game wouldn't have to spend thousands of dollars, and could get a feel for the game, and generate revenue at the same time for the DAO (or ATMTA)

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: This merchant would/could essentially act as a benchmark for some basic ships and resources, with the DAO or ATMTA essentially being the "regulator", which is what we have atm already anyway.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: also, in regards to increasing food req for non-scanning + non-mining ships, without giving those ship classes some other form of utility, is a pretty bad idea. At the very least, increase SCORE emissions for these types of what will be essentially "useless" ships, so people holding them don't feel like the asset they put their hard earned cash into doesn't depreciate due to a decision like increasing food requirements for non-specialized ships.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: The bottom line is simple, a rare medium fighter should have equal value in SAGE as a rare medium scanner. As Lerinor so eloquently put it once "all ships are right now are a set of stats", and that is 100% true atm. a rare medium fighter is half the price of a rare medium scanner, meaning that a scanner, in practice, is already more valuable than the fighter (even if the fighter is more "efficient" than the scanner) increasing the value of the scanner, doesn't fix the problem, it just makes the problem worse. The problem will be solved by giving the fighter ship more value in a different area, so it's not competing against scanners and miners. And I fully understand that this is supposed to come into play with the launch of C4, which is why it doesn't make sense to launch this change pre-C4, that is my only observation.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
We're in a tricky moment in Labs history. Cause what we really need is different utility for different ships, thus wrecking the narrative that fighters are cheap/scanners expensive, and so on.
But it doesn't arrive out of a sudden, and while it's developed, disparities will exist.

Scanners don't necessarily need a boost. It's fighters that do everything better than every other ship (except freighting).

But if you nerf fighters without combat, then the solution would actually create a bigger problem.

Considering how close C4 apparently is (from comments and the overall vibe), I would honestly not mess with scanners cooldown.

The food upkeep removal is great, though, and definitely needs balancing through increased burning elsewhere. Hence why scanners don't need a cooldown cut, because they'll indirectly already become better compared to fighters, that will suffer a food consumption increase.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: Also, keeping in mind that the only real bottleneck for Atlas emissions is SDU. Since it's uncapped, raising data runners output (essentially by almost 100%) would inflate emissions even further, since those extra SDUs would for sure end up in FIC redemption.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: I agree. And they already were. Have been for a while. And markets adjusted accordingly. I'm not saying they shouldn't be even better. They should provide all kinds of crazy bonuses! Critical rates, % increase in an area around the, etc. etc. Problem is, the timing. And the amount of buff that was granted.  Econ team has been very conservative on a lot of moves/levers, but all of a sudden we get crazy swings that destabilize things drastically. Atlas emissions are at ATH, and we have no SDU cap. Guess where all the extra SDUs from a 50% cooldown cut will go? FICs. Baaam, more Atlas inflation.  I mean... it's decided, and done. But especially on these particular matters, I'd expect a bottlenecking move towards SDU emissions, not the other way around.  (And this has nothing to do with personal assets/strategies. It's a genuine concern towards our current economical situation, which is dire).

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: We don't actually need a bottleneck on SDUs. They already are the bottleneck. What we need is a shift in SDU contribution to SB upgrades over FICs, which in turn will open up more opportunities to players for SB upgrades, a valuable resource sink. Which is why we're also rolling out additional changes to FICs.

Overall I like the patch, although I got lowered by 10% of my profit with fic2 change, but it looks fair, we are good here. No more food upkeep for crafting - very good. If you check "food_burned_graph" there is 2 players scanning only with DR and rest guys are playing with small/medium fighters and this is BIG amount of ships involved, so either they are OGs who bought this ships on primary or secondary market, or they crafted alot of mambas. 
Scanning is unlike mining, a competitive environment, a patch just took part of the SDU from one players and gave it to others, I would prefer that such initiatives were less painful (with the release of C4, for example)

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I agree that secondary pricing has significance. But market pricing is highly volatile, driven by sentiment and emotions, which change dramatically over time, and is influenced by a number of exogenous variables. We place a value on game assets (origination and vwap) and use that as an anchor point for functionality, power, and balance.   In this instance, reviewing data on demand of assets, it was very apparent that there was a lack of value proposition for Data Runners. This is independent of secondary pricing of various roles of ships. Fighter ships do not have their direct utility loop in the game, but it is being worked on in C4. And that is combat. If (when?) values of fighter ships go up on secondary because they have their utility function, it doesn't mean we're going to then nerf them just because their secondary perceived value went up. We're balancing based on performance within their loop. I would argue fighter ships are strongly overpowered in mining and freighting. (Alpha?) Those metrics will be nerfed to some extent when combat comes out because fighter ships will then have their dedicated loop -- I'm sure this just opened a can of worms.   Otherwise, I'm unclear as to what your particular issue with this update is. It isn't about making one role of ship better than another role of ship, or increasing secondary values on the marketplace. It is about creating the desired and intended utility for a specialized ship role given that role has a dedicated loop in the game right now. These economic changes could be made now. The DR has a specialized role in the game right now. So we will roll out balance changes as necessary to address current functionality as appropriate, versus waiting for a major patch release with a lot of new features.

author: [SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ [SKR]
content:
General Context:,Players are discussing the lack of functional distinction between ship classes. Right now, every ship is seen as just a “stat container” you pay real money for, without meaningful differentiation in gameplay roles.

---

 Main Points Raised:,Not all ship roles have a utility loop in the game yet (e.g., fighters currently serve no purpose).,Only Data Runners and Miners currently have actual in-game utility, which drives their higher market value.,There’s disagreement about the recent 2x boost to Data Runners — some argue Miners should have been improved instead.,Suggestions include:,
  Having miners consume toolkits (logical: maintenance).Making scanners consume food, or alternatively, removing food cost from miners for balance.,,
---

 Economy & Balancing:,Some point out that secondary market prices already reflect utility, even if devs don’t use it as an official metric.,ATMTA (the devs) respond that:,
  The goal is to assign a meaningful loop to each ship type.Balancing is based on current functionality, not market prices.,Once C4 (combat) is released, fighters will finally have a role. For now, changes target ships with active loops, like scanners.,,
---

 Community Frustration:,Certain players (especially “[HEIM] SDU whore”) express frustration because:,
  Only select ship types are being reworked.There’s uncertainty around when C4 will arrive.,The system seems to reward only specific ships, making them unaffordable or unfairly prioritized.,,
---

 Economic Considerations:,With fixed ATLAS emissions, if one ship’s ROI increases, others inherently earn less → imbalance.,Food consumption will be proportional to VWAP, but it’s unclear which VWAP value is being used.,
---

thanks IA

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: FWIW, I still don't like the idea of auctions for initial rentals. As frustrating as the experience is now, I think auctions on the frontend sets us up for failure in the future. Ideally inventory increases over time, and people are able to snag a rental immediately if they're interested in trying out SA or SAGE. I do, however, like the idea of an auction system that can run in the background on outstanding rentals and convert to the next highest bidder on renewal.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content: this does NOT work.  If there is an edge to be made then no system will ever be always equal.  If you make a "native bot" then someone will just make a non native bot FASTER then your native bot.  Winning by .00001 second is still winning.  And then you will then end up in a war of attrition and i guarantee you will lose.  If people want to be successful then they have to CONTINUE being succesful.  The other guy is working a lot harder, and thus he is going to win.  Whether its through automation or any other tactic where there is a winner and there is a loser.  This game is very much like the real world.  Adapt or die.  I know from personal experience, I've been battling 1000+ other validators who are constantly trying to steal my spot.  ITS CONSTANT PVP.   No one is going to help me.  So I just keep fighting til I can't or I lose.  Key word: Keep fighting.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Agreed, but the current situation is: Not everyone can program a bot or has access to a bot. For these player it is not "adapt or die", but "die or die" (to put it somewhat exaggeratedly). Btw, there are ways to prevent that 3rd party bots have an edge over the native bot. E.g. the contract has a delay. In the first 5 seconds only the native bot can accept a rental (via a signed message), after 5 seconds it is free for all. But yes, some people won't like this, too, because it is a somewhat centralized solution. Conclusion: There is no easy solution.

reply.preview: Agreed, but the current situation is: Not everyone can program a bot or has access to a bot. author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: You could hire a service to rent for you.  Preferably one that is running automation.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: I'm also wondering if its even worth creating a service like this, because a service like this is only valuable as long as rental ships stay in demand.  The minute we get liquidity then any systems that are created to rent ships fast are essentially dead.  Is it worth that risk?

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
And fwiw I don't actually think rentals are going to get "new players" in.  They aren't really sophisticated enough to know what to do or how to value rentals.  They will likely lose money renting.

Instead, they should be buying a 5-60$ ship.  Starting there, figuring out the game.  And then going to rentals.  (Where they are no longer new).

Or starting with Holosim and progressing.  Again, no longer "new."

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: I bet I can't share a youtube video here, but the honest truth about bots is that it's super hard to catch "cheaters" with math. And well that's all we can do with programming. With that being said, it's also easy to have an opinion about something that doesn't represent the actual statistics. Are there currently 0 rentals on the marketplace? Are there rentals at market right now? Bots will have an unfair advantage "taking those sweet deals" just like if you had 1,000 humans pressing claim on a Taylor Swift ticket. For bots and the overall health. It's all about supply and market. If every rental was at market (not a single on below it) bots wouldn't be botting right?.. With all those said, I doubt I could persuade my little brother into SAGE and I think that should be the goal. Not ensuring everyone can have the best deal possible at all times.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: What about ATMTA inventory rented at a fixed rate, smaller sized fleets, just enough to kick-start the experience for new players, but with a twist: restrict them to LRZ, somehow. This would make these fleets completely unattractive to us here, it would be sought after, but new players could start their experience.  Maybe even create a quest-line for a specific fleet composition (mine 1m carbon, deliver to CSS, craft 200k graphene, sell to NPC). And then as a reward, they get back what they paid in Atlas for the rental. Closed system, no inflation, no extraction, great onboarding experience.  Speaking for myself, I'd absolutely go for something like this if I was entering a new unknown and unfamiliar ecosystem, knowing that I wouldn't lose money if I completed the full quest line, and would be in a much more knowledgeable position after it's over, to make informed decisions should I decide to acquire assets.

author: Kr1gs
content:
2 cents on the Planets/Resources discussion. IMO I’m not overly focused on if a faction has more resources/planet. 

As I interpret some of the between the lines data, while a faction may have more resources per planet and more planets overall, the location of resources may not be conducive to logistical operations. 

I.e yeah a faction may have a ton of shit in everywhere, but if it’s not optimal or disorganized it becomes more chaotic. Additionally, that means that players in said faction will have to travel more between the various planets/nodes because they are more spread out

author: Kr1gs
content: At least that’s how I would game design balance between resource density and resource quantity

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: One of my talking notes for metaverse nomads was "the death of LP.".   The latest econ report shows that mud/Ustur make twice as much LP as Oni players do.  Yup, Oni players are working twice as hard and getting paid half for it.  And this has been going on for a year+.  It was a good run and a fun experiment but I'll be very very happy when it's gone.  For anyone paying attention this is me not defending ATMTA.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Exactly my point and why I don't like the LP system. Players benefit by having LESS players participate.  If you can convince everyone that LP is awful you could theoretically get 100% of the rewards.  It's not (currently) in players interest to participate in an LP loop once a certain threshhold of players have been hit.  And a min/maxing player would/should actively discourage other players from picking up the mantle.  Oni just has some real brave souls who have been actively earning less income then other factions just to keep Starbases upgrading.   And who knows if it will pay off. (e.g. in C4 with potentially upgraded Starbases.)

reply.preview: Exactly my point and why I don't like the LP system. Players benefit by having LESS players participate. author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
But now that I'm thinking about this a bit more indepth, its entirely possible this scenario occured simply because Starbased was a limited module. The rewards for upgrading Starbases was just too small. 

In C4, faction cooperation and faction driven goals will likely result in massively more rewards then in Starbased, through conquering, combat, and  coordination.  But if the rewards for a faction don't scale significantly with the SUCCESS of that faction, I feel we will end up in the same place.

So theres a good chance that SDUs production will not in fact, go up.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content: My guess is that it’s the simplest way to move food demand from a global upkeep system to being proportional to individual production. Food upkeep per crew would probably be the ideal, but that’s likely a big overhaul. Where as cranking up the food on mining ships does close to the same thing and all they have to do is flip bases to %100 speed and remove the global upkeep. That said  those who buy raw and craft got a pretty sweet buff.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: I believe you're actually losing more this way. Even if you were loading food entirely on your own, you were getting 120 million LP for 20 million food. That helped you recover nearly 80% of the food you spent. But now, all the food you use is coming entirely out of your own resources, and you're not gaining anything in return. You're one of the biggest whales in the game — producing food might not be a big challenge for you, but we don’t have that kind of advantage.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
The issue here isn't about supporting small investors — it's clear that no real effort is being made for them.
If the real intention is to support small investors, then they should follow a roadmap like this:
Reduce the food and ammo requirements for XXS and XS ships. That way, these ships can start generating profit again.
At the same time, if they increase the requirements for larger ships in a reasonable and balanced way, the gap in profitability would be evened out.
But right now, they are not doing that — the requirements for XXS ships remain unchanged.

I always reinvested every penny (erm, I mean Atlas), because I think SA will have a bright future.
Maybe this was a mistake, maybe it was the right thing to do ... time will tell.  
Regarding the discussion Oni, Mud, Ustur: I think Mud and Ustur are very attractive for players who want to extract, because you'll get more Atlas when you do SB upgrades (less players = bigger share from the pool) . As long as the game is designed like this, I can't blame anyone and it is what it is.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: i just read up on the specifics. the distro is split evenly between each node same as it is now. but now you need 1m on your account regardless of how many nodes you have to get the full amount (200 gala per node). those that don't have 1m get rewarded proportional to how close to 1m tokens they have. ie if you have 500k you get half the reward, and the other half is distributed among only those that have the full 1m.   if you're like the devs and have 100+ nodes you only need 10k gala per node which is nothing, these nodes not only would get get the full distro, they would also split the tokens that all the smaller node operators loose for not having enough tokens.  basically everyone has only 1 or 2 nodes.

author: Zaif
content: A lot of these games used buzzwords like "web3," "blockchain," and "crypto" thinking it would be easy money, but the games themselves were just bad. Most of them put in the bare minimum effort to keep their communities hopeful and convince them to spend.  They weren’t good web2 games. They didn’t even use web3 tech in any meaningful way.  This purge in web3 gaming was expected. Honestly, I thought it would have happened sooner. Selfishly, I’m excited about it. The number of games competing for attention is shrinking fast, and I’m seeing more new people joining us now than I did six months ago.

reply.preview: A lot of these games used buzzwords like "web3," "blockchain," and "crypto" thinking it would be easy money, but the games themselves were just bad. Most of them put in the bare minimum effort to keep their communities hopeful and convince them to spend. They weren’t good web2 games. They didn’t even use web3 tech in any meaningful way. This purge in web3 gaming was expected. Honestly, I thought it would have happened sooner. Selfishly, I’m excited about it. The number of games competing for attention is shrinking fast, and I’m seeing more new people joining us now than I did six months ago.

Enough exploits for you?

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: I have actually scored a few good deals too. The deals I have scored I believe are new rentals put on the market that day/hour. The botters seem to be set up to grab the already rented fleets the second they go back onto the market. So, for example, I had about 11 fleets rented for Zero 2 Hero series. I knew the exact second 8 of them were going to expire and going back out to rent. I was ready with my atlas, Sol, fast computer and speedy internet to re-rent. I got sniped on all 8 fleets. I'm down to only three fleets left. I also tried to rent a WHOLE bunch of fleets the second the became available using info from EVEEYE. Every fleet I tried for I was outbeaten in the same way. It seems very unlikely there is a crazy dude  waiting 24/7 to rent fleets AND can 100% of the time out-maneuver me when the buzzer hits. I could be wrong though.  I mostly brought this up because I guarantee that the botters are right here in the foundation room. Also the people who want star atlas to not suck are also right  here in the foundation room. Marketplace botters at least give us liquidity, even though they are getting filthy rich on ship sales. Ship rental botters are stealing from the entire community and do not give back anything.

author: ROME | Winston [ROME]
content:
⁠😎┃foundation-room⁠
Even without obfuscation of fleet movements, defenders do have the advantage of using the Starbase defenses. If they are still going with the SAGE manual, you can set your fleets in passive defense mode on a Starbase and your fleet will add up to the SB built-in defense systems. 
Now it depends how they balance the difficulty but a high tier SB might be a tough nut to crack, giving time for a faction to respond and send reinforcements.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
Problem is (from a technical POV):
You'll need a centralized entity that knows the (secret) target sector and the trajectory/flight path.
And this centralized entity accepts scanning attempts and reveals a ship if a scan was successful.
And because it is a centralized entity, it can't (fully) run on the blockchain.
So you'll end up with a game that not longer completely runs on the blockchain.
Stealth may be an important factor, but having a fully decentralized game is also an important factor.
While there are ways to keep a value secret on the blockchain, the implementation would always need a centralized factor, be it a special entity or be it a combined hash/key/zk-SNARKS system. Without a centralized factor, everything is public and you can just see what a ship is doing.
Of course, I would like to hear what the SA team thinks about this.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
@Definitely_Not_JayWalk to your question in FR about onboarding clarity, it's a fine balance and has come up here and there ongoing. For me, the balance is between:

The perception that nothing is launched yet.,People thinking there is more than is here so far (to be clear, there is a ton already here, I'm talking about "polished" game loops).,
Personally, I do believe it's better to at least give folks an idea of what the reality and challenges are today. This is not knocking on the project; we're simply in an alpha or even pre-alpha state, and as such certain things should be expected. I do believe this is where the mods are in a tough spot, because if we have things too "official", it also sends the message to players that what they see today is as expected. 

This topic of onboarding thus is a delicate one. We of course want new players, but in some ways getting too much exposure right now still is too early. This is why I come back to sharing some of the reality in a positive way, but being real with people about what they can or can't do, or what it costs, etc. This is why I previously wrote a serious of articles (I'm the one doing the Galia Crafters articles), to try and get some of that out there as a point-in-time reference. Of course, the challenge is keeping stuff evergreen since we see changes quite a lot.

The net of it for me is, we're almost purposefully in that gray area between fully embracing onboarding, and trying not to burn a large base right now. It's painful, but I'd prefer that we err on the side of not being too public right now until we get a little further.

author: Shaddix1 | Star Atlas Mod [AEP]
content: As a Project Manager myself here is what I would do Monday morning: 1) Form a working group of 20 different people together from the team from all different areas of development 2) Hold a 30min kickoff meeting with an end goal of developing an answer to following question: "Can we play the game yet?"  Create 1 short Discord ready response and 1 longer linked response. 3) Break the team into 4 groups of 5.  Give each team 3 hours to come up with a complete comprehensive answer that answers each of the above. 4) Regroup and have each group present their findings 5) Combine the best of each responses and develop one unified message 6) Have a follow-up working group where the team presents to solution to a select community group and recieves feedback which they either choose to act on, modify their response to or just take as feedback for future consideration. 7) Publish finalized standard response for Mods to post (or just have a pinned message to refer to)

author: defiantwyvern
content:
Having a good unified mod response would be great, but after that, attracting people to sage and having them actually engage is outside of their control.

Sage is a tough pill for any newcomer to swallow b/c of the investment required to have any meaningful earnings or impact on the economy. I see that as the major issue. The number of people I know that would play a UE5-like game vs the number that would get into a SAGE-like game is a lot different.

author: defiantwyvern
content: Out of all the non-SA people I know, I think literally 0 of them would be interested in getting into Sage as it stands right now. If it was free to play, some would probably try it, though.

author: defiantwyvern
content: People I talk to about it are interested to hear about it, but that's about as far as it goes. Most people are scared off by putting a ton of money into a game.

content: Star Atlas is in the "Adaptive challenge phase".  There is no known winning formula to scale.  It's a normal phase to go through.  Best approach is: MASSIVE amounts of low cost experiments. author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
Here's a stab at a potential brief, crisp new player overview to at least get them somewhat oriented.

-----

Hi there, and welcome to the Star Atlas community! We're glad you're here, and encourage you to explore the getting started guides [insert link to channels]. There's a lot going on, and a TON being built out. Think of us as currently in an alpha state, but with thousands of active players already participating.

There's a couple of major game loops to participate in today; these are the Star Atlas game in the Epic Store (referred to by some in our community as the Unreal Engine game or UE5), as well as the browser-based live-economy strategy game (referred to as SAGE). Here's a quick description of each:

Star Atlas game in the Epic Store: This is a playable shooter/racer/showroom where you can play individually or with others! We are constantly making updates, and this will give you a good feel for the foundation of our Star Atlas metaverse. Getting started is extremely easy, and you can get going in minutes without needing to own anything in-game. You can access it here [insert instructions here on getting a key].

Browser-Based "SAGE" Strategy Game: SAGE is where players interact with our current live game economy, with the ability to manage fleets, crew, resources and more. You can use fleets and crew to mine, transport, craft and build. Everything is on-chain, which means it's a real economy where you interact with thousands of other players. Participating in SAGE requires ships and crew, and if you're interested, check out more resources here [insert resources].

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I'll have to disagree here, and I'd like to bring up the example of EVE Online — a game where stealth mechanics have been refined over decades of gameplay.  In EVE, there are ships with covert cloaking devices, and they're nearly impossible to detect while cloaked. The only reliable way to find them is to physically bump into them — which only works if you saw the exact spot where they cloaked and managed to reach it before they left. There is no dedicated “anti-stealth” mechanic in the game.  But that doesn’t mean a cloaked ship is an instant win.  While cloaked, you're completely limited in what you can do: you can’t attack, target enemies, scan, mine, or interact with anything. You can only move and observe. It’s a tool for recon, ambush, and tactical positioning — not for direct engagement.  And the moment you decloak, enemies can immediately lock onto you — and once you're targeted, you can’t cloak again. To escape, you either need to outrun them (while being slowed down), or destroy everyone who's got a lock on you — which, let’s be honest, rarely happens.  And here’s the key point:  Every time a game tries to introduce “anti-stealth” mechanics — whether it’s probes, sensors, or other countermeasures — it completely ruins the purpose of stealth. A well-equipped or numerically superior opponent will simply hunt down and kill the cloaked ship preemptively, denying it any chance to play its role. Stealth turns from a strategic tool into a fragile and pointless gimmick.  The bottom line: stealth isn't overpowered — it’s a trade-off. You sacrifice action for safety. But if stealth can be “countered” by brute force or scanning tools, then it ceases to exist as a meaningful mechanic at all.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content: yeah on the topic of all the recent "web3" gaming failures, I've looked at all or most of them, and candidly their approaches were all build a game, then put in blockchain elements. That's kind of just slapping a web3 label on what's already being done. SA's approach (to me at least) is different because it's from the ground up, at least for SAGE. Everything we do in SAGE is only possible because of the inherent blockchain, from the core gameplay to the daily tools we use. Building the bones on chain vs slapping the chain on top of a game is night and day.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
The nice thing is that even today, the econ loop needs several layers of "skills" (additive), to be absolutely maximizing earnings/efficiency. But, the good news is also that one doesn't need any of that depth to get going. 

If you want to mine hydrogen, just do that. Still positive ROI. 
If you want to strategize LP, or the entire supply chain it takes to get to LP, do that. 

The ROI goes up for each layer of complexity a player is willing (and can) take on. I feel combat will be similar. So, in the end, more "mathing"/sleuthing/thinking/planning means better outcomes and more rewards (regardless of fleet size). Thus has it ever been in SAGE, thus shall it ever be (I hope)...

Who here saw/read the Hunger Games?

Piggybacking off the aforementioned suggestions, why not have people pay for certain items, and then allowing them to control a drone (or something similar) to drop it at or near their target hero?

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
Instead of seeing pay 2 win, @Icy, you could choose to see the economy embracing these UE5 gameplay modes 

You can always limit the amount of items that can be dropped and/or randomize the items a drone operator can buy from the “shop”. 

Note that there is skill involved in dropping these as well, and making sure they end up with the right player. Perhaps they can even be shot from the sky.

P2W aspects can be easily mitigated. That said, introducing economic aspects into the game will naturally introduce some of that.
As long as we stay cognizant of that, we can hopefully fine tune it so it does not have a great impact.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: You could probably learn a thing or two by studying how Entropia Universe's marketplace works, it's quite genius actually. There is an expiry on orders, and the fee is taken upon listing an item(people are unable to remove their listing, it either gets purchased, or expires), and regardless of end result, ATMTA gets their fee, bots get removed, and to add to that, there is a double price option, where one price is a minimum price, that people can auction up through bidding wars, or there is a buy now option that people can just buy. It's not your classic orderbook system, but highly effective against botting.

author: [SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ [SKR]
content: I think so, too. It makes no sense to buy an IMP instead of Taps. An IMP has a mining rate of 183.477, a Tap 19.192 So 9.56 Taps have the same mining rate as an IMP. But when I look at the food consumption: Taps: 9.56 * 0.38 = 3.633 IMP: 5.297 So the IMP consumes 45% more food than the Taps! And the Taps are even cheaper! (2430*9.56 = 23231 vwap vs 27500 vwap). Wtf? Why even buy an IMP now?? Honestly, who came up with these numbers? (the only thing that MAY justify an IMP is the increased cargo room, but this doesn't play a role if you e.g. do stationary mining)

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
@Santi thanks for the reply in the FR. Bringing it here to keep FR declutered.

Agree to all you said. It's more delicate, but I think it can be done positively, and without over-sharing stuff (or leaking stuff prematurely). Ugh, that sounded dirty... 

There's great potential to having a few hardcore players looking for inconsistencies, potential exploits, etc.

For example: this whole FIC rebalancing/mechanics changes. We talked about it a lot in Aephia, and could predict how much it would be exploited. And the theory-crafting solution we arrived at was exactly what was implemented on the last update.

All I'm saying is, there are devoted people really interested in helping to make SA pristine/flawless.
Use us. NDAs galore, we don't mind.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: Exactly my thoughts. Larger ships should be more efficient. In the past this was the case, because larger ships burned less food and less ammo. Now they only burn less ammo. But the Imps don't burn ammo at all, so suddenly a small ship is more efficient than a large ship (when doing stationary mining). I get it that they wanted to make a new use case for food, because the SB upkeep is not longer needed, but making the food consumption absolutely linear was a bad idea, imo. It also doesn't make sense from a crew perspective: Airbike 1 crew and 0.0014 food. Superphoenix 347 crew and 70 food consumption (= 0.2 food per crew member). So the SP crew eats 143x more food. That's ridiculous.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Maybe you have different numbers than me. But when I look at the mining rate, small ships used to burn much more food and more or less the same fuel/ammo. So they were less efficient. Here are the old values: E.g. X5, 0.99 MR, 0.029 food, 0.05 ammo, 48 fuel/AU Butch, 30 MR, 0.215 food, 1.464 ammo, 1543 fuel/AU You need 30 X5 for the same mining rate. Food: 0.029 * 30 = 0.87 food (that's more than 4x more food!) Ammo: 0.05 * 30 = 1.5 ammo (more or less the same) Fuel: 48 * 30 = 1440 fuel (more or less the same) And for transporters fuel/cargo is the crucial value and here large ships are far superior: Maxhog: 2200 cargo, 37 fuel/AU Sledbarge: 608838 cargo, 3710 fuel/AU You need 277 maxhogs for the same cargo room. Fuel: 37 * 277 = 10249 fuel/AU, so you burn ~3x more fuel per cargo unit.

Food: 0.029 * 30 = 0.87 food (that's more than 4x more food!) Fuel: 37 * 277 = 10249 fuel/AU, so you burn ~3x more fuel per cargo unit.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: @SW4GN3R sorry for the tag, but I think it would be cool to have a potential correlation analysis on this specific comment I made a while back.  SDU emissions were bound to go up with the DR update, not down. And it happened as predicted (even though scanning is now less attractive with non-DR). How is the Atlas emissions after the update?  We had a hit in the economy due to increased food costs (not complaining, just a statement), and it possibly affected the total mined R9 (due to less renting, and smaller margins).  Now that bases craft all at 100%, FIC crafting times got reduced across the board. With more SDUs being found, would that possibly translate into more total FICs being crafted, and thus a higher Atlas emission?  (Maybe that's a question to Chris, but you were here commenting about SDUs, and I felt it was appropriate to tag you. If not, I'm sorry).  Also, excited about the announcement on the announcement of a Town Hall!

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: Could potentially result in more FIC crafting, and higher emissions. Yes. But it, so far, has resulted in about a 10% increase in daily SDU discovery. So the major driving force in ATLAS emissions still relies on player choice between Starbase upgrading and FIC crafting.  edit: We're also seeing a modest decrease in the number of scanning fleets, driven by the increase in food cost. By and large, I would say this is by design. Our goal was to drive genuine utility to data runners, and I suspect the people that are still participating in that loop own DRs, and are now earning a higher portion of total SDUs. So in a lot of ways, it also dependso n what those players decide what to do with their SDUs: FICs, SB Upgrades, or sell.  edit 2: People (maybe you) were assuming that the 50% reduction in cooldown time would lead to a 2x increase in SDU discovery for DRs, which I argued against. Anecdotally, looking at my on DR fleets, I'm seeing about a 30-50% increase in SDU discovery. Which makes sense, because SDUs are largely RNG, and not linear like other resource production assets.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: Nearly impossible for me to say right now. Perfect world, the player in UE will have access to earnings mechanisms that outperform those automated systems in SAGE. Probably more RNG. Perhaps more risk. Although the assumption of playing 24/7 doesn’t work unless they’re hiring people to operate that fleet when they are offline, or if it’s just being used to benchmark ROI. Nevertheless, if it’s possible to provide greater rewards for someone directly managing a single asset in UE vs. automating a fleet in SAGE/FC, that would be the target. But we will have to see how that evolves with UE dev, and whether or not the potential for exploitation exists.

author: [Peace DAC] TGI LizardMUD
content: The price can be worrying sure, but knowing that there will be an end date for POLIS selling from ATMTA, lots of polished products still to be released, Season 0, Star Atlas is a fully on-chain game... you could think of this time as a great time to decentralize POLIS to as many players or dedicated community members as possible. Then scarce asset mechanics can start to kick in, 4 years before the token emissions ends.   There are only more distributed POLIS emission if POLIS stakers sell their POLIS rewards, but if they don't sell... Then the only available supply are those on exchanges. Low token price, lots of new products (demand), very little sellers, lots of committed community holders, no VC dumping... Some bright thoughts on this Friday  31.5% of POLIS is locked in the locker currently

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
Honestly if the team creates a full panet with the same quality that they do for all other assets, with lots of cool places to explore, fly your ships, run around with your crew in and maybe a few moons and other more polished but barren planets that players can seamlessly fly to, do missions for, play with other players, fight NPCs... etc With all of the mechanics ATMTA has built so far, it could honestly turn heads, especially when you add on-chain: progression, Commercial and Residential Real Estate, Combat Racing, Character Creation, a full Racing Career, Fighter Career, Ship Skin Crafting, Character and Account Progression, Onchain Event Scheduler and Mission Generator, Onchain Ship Configuration, Ship Mastery, Additional Ship Roles, Tiers 0-5 Racer (ONI/MUD/Ustur), Punaab Race... not even considering a full Fleet Command C4 upgrade.

I think the team could easily have a fully Solana on-chain version and if needed a fully free play version for regular Web2 players to dip their toes playing that would run on ATLAS Net with zero gas fees.

Just look at everything that is coming and if even half of it releases in the first half of next year, do you not think they are already working on at least the UE5 portions?
Runway to Season 0 is all that matters, plus cash for marketing to bring the product to market IMO

author: ZiggyMUD
content: Star Atlas is being priced as a blockchain browser game with 1000 players and a few UE5 modes. The magic happens when everyone sees the depth that SAGE players do, but in a game like Fleet Command with a fantastic UI/UX and a larger UE5 third person world with lots of things to do. Most projects in the space focus on the UI some on the UX but almost none focus on the gameplay mechanics, the repeatable gameplay. Star Atlas is just built differently

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: This is just a hunch that I have had for some time now and could be completely wrong, FYI  The team has been cooking. There are about 50-60 workers that are working on UE5 alone. If anyone here can tell me what the other 15-25 Sperasoft workers have been building im all ears.   My estimates from past conversations  7 are on characters,7 on animations, VFX, guns, lighting,5 are on ships,2 Environment,1 Planet tech,1 Shypto,1 Brad,1 Gary,Maybe 5 for testing and bugs,Maybe 5 concept designers, We have seen content coming in Season 1 like the ONI CSS and the materials that will be used, mining mechanics, trame system (finished before but still in Season 1 for ONI CSS) We help test flight, hover, crew mechanics, planet tech, servers, blockchain connections to UE5, SAGE which is core gameplay mechanics (very important)... We have seen very little crew or ship skins since Nov/Dec 2023 We have not seen anything of the Ioki Open World (not even grey box), yet there is going to be a full planet with villages/cities, USTUR specific ownable habs, new USTUR textures, caves, temples, trees, wildlife... "explore the lore, environment, and culture of the Ustur Species"  We have seen things from Season 1 but little updates for things coming in Season 0 which "could" be coming in the first half of 2026    Why would ATMTA hired the team of 2 UE5 environment workers to start working on the ONI CSS (Season 1) over a year ago if a team in Sperasoft was not already working on some of the larger Season 0 builds aka Ioki Open World Brad started working on mining which is not planned for public release until Season 1. Very fun mechanic but again this will likely be polished in Season 1 as per the Roadmap.

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
In July 2023 the team thought they would have enough runway to last them 12-18 months. We are now going on 22 months from that point with likely 12ish months of runway in hand at the current team size burn rates, without including the current ongoing cash flows of nearly $500,000 USDC monthly. So we are talking about 36+ months of runway from July 2023 and this extends every day. Not bad at all. And from a runway point of view we were here before, with way less products in the pipeline, no advanced blockchain build/networking infra, no shooter, the racer was not great yet, we didn't have the two passionate level designers, the team had no clue if the SAGE economy would work, characters, movement, customizations for ships and crew where nowhere close... Star Atlas is in a different spot completely compared to July 2023 because this community believed and came together. 

Also consider that we still have a very large core community that cares about Star Atlas with the current gameplay, and this will likely only improve over the next 6-12 months with the addition of all the coming product releases.
Don't forget about the other two trailers already done, CORE season 3, Tulle AI tools...

Star Atlas has never had a publicly released game product, that should be released in the next 12 months 
No one is considering any revenue from this release in either a free to play model or fully on-chain model. Star Atlas has never been able to show off its complex on-chain economy in high fidelity, no UE5 gameplay tied to the economy, no on-chain TPS reward systems (not just the rewards based on the top leaderboard players). 

Also another thing to consider, as more projects start moving to the polishing stage or going public, more workers will be moved to Season 0 content/ships (Hero, Opod,etc.)

Pre-Season is about game foundation building (still lots of fun) but Season 0 is when the team flexes their muscles IMO

We just all need to stay positive and realize whats coming
